PubliclyAccessible.^description: Unless you intend for your cluster to be publicly accessible, the cluster should not be configured with PubliclyAccessible set to true

EncryptedInTransit.^description: TLS can be used to help prevent potential attackers from using person-in-the-middle or similar attacks to eavesdrop on or manipulate network traffic. Only encrypted connections over TLS should be allowed. Encrypting data in transit can affect performance. You should test your application with this feature to understand the performance profile and the impact of TLS

MaintenanceWindow.^description: Configure maintenance windows during off-peak hours to minimize impact on your workloads. A well-planned maintenance window ensures system updates and patches are applied with minimal disruption to your operations.

AutomaticSnapshots.^description: Backups help you to recover more quickly from a security incident. They strengthen the resilience of your systems. Amazon Redshift takes periodic snapshots by default. Automatic snapshots should be enabled and retained for at least seven days.

CrossRegionSnapshots.^description: Cross-region snapshot copying provides disaster recovery capabilities by maintaining backup copies in a different AWS Region. This helps ensure business continuity in case of regional failures.

AuditLogging.^description: Amazon Redshift audit logging provides additional information about connections and user activities in your cluster. This data can be stored and secured in Amazon S3 and can be helpful in security audits and investigations.

AutomaticUpgrades.^description: Enabling automatic major version upgrades ensures that the latest major version updates to Amazon Redshift clusters are installed during the maintenance window. These updates might include security patches and bug fixes. Keeping up to date with patch installation is an important step in securing systems.

EnhancedVpcRouting.^description: Enhanced VPC routing forces all COPY and UNLOAD traffic between the cluster and data repositories to go through your VPC. You can then use VPC features such as security groups and network access control lists to secure network traffic. You can also use VPC Flow Logs to monitor network traffic.

DefaultAdminUsername.^description: When creating a Redshift cluster, you should change the default admin username to a unique value. Default usernames are public knowledge and should be changed upon configuration. Changing the default usernames reduces the risk of unintended access. You can't change the admin username for your Amazon Redshift cluster after it is created. To create a new cluster, follow the instructions in the link provided.

DefaultDatabaseName.^description: When creating a Redshift cluster, you should change the default database name to a unique value. Default names are public knowledge and should be changed upon configuration. As an example, a well-known name could lead to inadvertent access if it was used in IAM policy conditions. You can't change the database name for your Amazon Redshift cluster after it is created. For instructions on creating a new cluster, see Getting started with Amazon Redshift in the Amazon Redshift Getting Started Guide

EncryptedAtRest.^description: In Amazon Redshift, you can turn on database encryption for your clusters to help protect data at rest. When you turn on encryption for a cluster, the data blocks and system metadata are encrypted for the cluster and its snapshots. Encryption of data at rest is a recommended best practice because it adds a layer of access management to your data. Encrypting Redshift clusters at rest reduces the risk that an unauthorized user can access the data stored on disk.

EncryptedWithKMS.^description: To help protect data at rest, ensure encryption with AWS Key Management Service (AWS KMS) is enabled for your Amazon Redshift cluster. Because sensitive data can exist at rest in Redshift clusters, enable encryption at rest to help protect that data.
ref: [Database encryption for Amazon Redshift using AWS KMS]<https://docs.aws.amazon.com/redshift/latest/mgmt/working-with-db-encryption.html#working-with-aws-kms>

ReservedNode.^description: Amazon Redshift offers reserved nodes, which are a cost-effective way to operate your data warehouse by allowing you to reserve compute capacity for a one or three-year period. With reserved nodes, you can receive a significant discount compared to pay-as-you-go pricing. This can help you reduce your overall costs while maintaining the flexibility to scale compute capacity up or down based on your workload needs.

UnderutilisedCluster.^description: An underutilized Amazon Redshift cluster can lead to unnecessary costs. It's important to monitor your cluster usage and right-size your cluster based on your workload requirements. You can use Amazon Redshift metrics, such as CPU utilization, disk space usage, and query performance, to determine if your cluster is underutilized.

IAMRoles.^description: Use Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) through IAM roles to manage permissions effectively and adhere to the principle of least privilege. IAM roles are essential for secure integration with other AWS services (like S3, KMS, CloudWatch) and eliminate the need for storing long-term credentials. Roles provide temporary credentials that automatically rotate, enabling secure access while maintaining audit trails. Missing or improperly configured IAM roles can lead to security vulnerabilities, operational limitations, and compliance issues.

AZRelocation.^description: Amazon Redshift clusters are distributed across multiple availability zones. When you relocate a cluster, you can choose to relocate it to a new availability zone or to the same availability zone. Relocating a cluster to a new availability zone can help distribute your workload across multiple availability zones and improve performance.

VpcDeployment.^description: Validate cluster is deployed in VPC for network isolation. AWS recommends launching all Redshift clusters in Amazon VPC for enhanced security and network isolation. Clusters deployed in EC2-Classic lack the security features provided by VPC, such as security groups, network ACLs, and VPC Flow Logs.

AdvisorRecommendations.^description: Check for unaddressed Redshift Advisor recommendations for performance optimization. Amazon Redshift Advisor provides automated recommendations to help you optimize your cluster for better performance and lower costs. Advisor analyzes your workload and provides specific recommendations for table design, compression, query patterns, and cluster configuration. Addressing high-impact recommendations can significantly improve query performance and reduce operational costs.

EventNotifications.^description: Validate SNS event notifications are configured for cluster events. Amazon SNS notifications help you stay informed about important cluster events such as maintenance windows, failures, configuration changes, and snapshot operations. Configuring event notifications enables proactive monitoring and faster incident response, improving operational visibility and reducing downtime.

QueryMonitoringRules.^description: Validate Query Monitoring Rules are configured in WLM for query performance tracking. Query Monitoring Rules (QMR) allow you to automatically log or abort queries that exceed defined thresholds for metrics like execution time, rows returned, or disk usage. Implementing QMR helps identify problematic queries, prevent resource exhaustion, and maintain consistent cluster performance.

SecurityGroups.^description: Validate VPC security groups are attached to cluster. VPC security groups act as virtual firewalls to control inbound and outbound traffic to your Redshift cluster. Attaching security groups is essential for network access control and helps restrict connections to trusted sources. Note: This check validates security group attachment only; it does not inspect security group rules (which would require EC2 API access).
